g.data-narration: Welcome to Symbolic Reasoning Under Uncertainty. We'll explore how AI handles incomplete and ambiguous information.
Uncertainty in AI arises when information is incomplete, noisy, or ambiguous. Unlike classical logic with absolute truths, real-world problems have fuzzy boundaries.
g.text: Making decisions with incomplete, unreliable, or ambiguous information.
Unlike classical logic (true/false), uncertainty requires degrees of belief.

g.data-narration: Five main factors cause uncertainty: incomplete information, noisy data, ambiguity, changing environments, and expert knowledge limitations.
Non-monotonic reasoning differs from classical logic. Adding new information can invalidate previous conclusions, allowing for exceptions.
g.text: Traditional (Monotonic) Logic: Adding info NEVER invalidates conclusions.
Non-Monotonic: New evidence can retract previous conclusions.

g.data-narration: Classic example: Birds typically fly. Tweety is a bird, so Tweety flies. But if we learn Tweety is a penguin, we retract the conclusion.
When penguin knowledge is added, the exception rule triggers, making Tweety abnormal, which retracts the flying conclusion.
Default reasoning makes assumptions about typical cases when specific information is lacking. If X is a bird and can consistently fly, assume flies.
g.text: Making assumptions about typical cases when specific information is lacking.
Rule format: "If P is true, and it's consistent to assume Q, then conclude Q"

g.data-narration: In Prolog, default rules use negation-as-failure. When no exception exists, the default conclusion holds. Penguins trigger the exception.
Truth Maintenance Systems track belief dependencies. Justification-based TMS records reasons for beliefs; assumption-based TMS manages assumptions.
g.text: Track dependencies between beliefs and maintain consistency when adding new information.
Records reasons for beliefs to enable retraction

g.data-narration: TMS example: Car starts belief depends on battery OK AND fuel adequate. If battery fails, TMS retracts the car starts belief.
g.text: (Node C: depends on A AND B)

g.data-narration: Fuzzy logic handles uncertainty using degrees of truth between 0 and 1, rather than binary true/false. It represents vague concepts naturally.
g.text: Uses degrees of truth between 0 and 1, not binary true/false.

g.data-narration: Example: Temperature membership. At 22°C, cold=0, warm=0.8, hot=0.0. Fuzzy logic smoothly transitions between these linguistic categories.
Compare the approaches: Non-monotonic handles exceptions, default reasoning makes assumptions, fuzzy logic handles degrees, TMS maintains consistency.
Key takeaways: Symbolic methods maintain explainability while handling uncertainty through non-monotonicity, defaults, fuzzy logic, and truth maintenance.
g.text: • Symbolic uncertainty = rules/logic + uncertainty handling mechanisms
• Non-monotonic: Conclusions can be retracted with new evidence
• Default: Assumes typical cases, withdraws when exceptions found
• Fuzzy: Degrees between 0 and 1 for vague concepts

g.data-narration: Real-world applications: Expert systems for medical diagnosis, fuzzy controllers for robotics, NLP for ambiguous language, and legal reasoning systems.
g.text: Expert Systems • Fuzzy Control • NLP • Legal Reasoning
